subtopic_detail: Sentinel facial features in foetal alcohol spectrum disorder assessment
stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a community clinic reviewing a 4-year-old boy referred by his kindergarten teacher for behavioural concerns and slow language. His birth mother has been open about ongoing alcohol use through the pregnancy. He is in long-term foster care. His foster mother reports impulsivity, poor emotion regulation, and difficulty following two-step instructions. Growth is below the 3rd centile for weight and length. There is no history of perinatal infection, head trauma, or seizures, and metabolic screening done at school entry was normal.

data_table.Growth: Weight 13.1 kg (<3rd centile), height 95 cm (<3rd centile), head circumference 47.5 cm (<3rd centile)
data_table.Facial findings: Short palpebral fissures, smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border
data_table.Development: Receptive language 2 years 6 months, expressive language 2 years, gross motor age-appropriate
data_table.Hearing and vision: Audiology and ophthalmology screening normal in the last 6 months

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in confirming the suspected diagnosis?

options.text: Send a chromosomal microarray and fragile X testing as the next investigation
options.rationale: Genetic testing is reserved for atypical features, additional dysmorphism, or when other syndromes are competing on the differential; the sentinel triad with confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure makes broad genetic testing low yield as the first step. (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD)
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: Refer for multidisciplinary neurodevelopmental assessment with standardised facial measurement
options.rationale: Australian diagnostic criteria require multidisciplinary assessment confirming three sentinel facial features and severe impairment in three or more neurodevelopmental domains, alongside documented prenatal exposure. (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD)
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: Commence a 6-week trial of methylphenidate for presumed attentional and impulse difficulties
options.rationale: Stimulant trials may be considered after formal diagnosis and behavioural strategies have failed, not as a diagnostic shortcut in a 4-year-old with developmental concerns. (Source: RCH CPG: ADHD)

options.text: Arrange brain MRI to exclude a structural cause of the developmental delay
options.rationale: Routine MRI is not part of the diagnostic pathway when the clinical picture and prenatal exposure are typical; imaging is reserved for focal neurology or seizures. (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD)
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: Reassure the family and re-review developmental progress in 6 months in clinic
options.rationale: Delaying assessment misses the early-intervention window that improves long-term outcomes when neurodevelopmental impairment is already evident. (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD)
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

explanation.summary: Foetal alcohol spectrum disorder is diagnosed in Australia using the Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD, which requires either three sentinel facial features (short palpebral fissures, smooth philtrum, thin upper lip vermilion) with confirmed or unknown prenatal alcohol exposure, OR severe neurodevelopmental impairment across three of ten domains with confirmed exposure. Multidisciplinary assessment is mandated. Early identification opens access to NDIS support, structured behavioural therapy, and educational accommodations. Stimulants may help comorbid attentional symptoms after diagnosis but are not the first response.
key_points: Three sentinel facial features plus exposure suffice for diagnosis of FASD with three sentinel features
Without all three facial features, severe impairment in 3 of 10 neurodevelopmental domains plus confirmed exposure is required
Growth restriction is supportive but not required for diagnosis
Multidisciplinary assessment is mandatory in the Australian framework
Early diagnosis unlocks NDIS and educational supports
explanation.pearls: Smooth philtrum and thin upper vermilion are scored against the University of Washington lip-philtrum guide, with rank 4 or 5 counting as a sentinel feature.

sources.label: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

subtopic_detail: Diagnostic pathway for suspected Hirschsprung disease in delayed meconium passage
stem: You are the paediatric registrar called to assess a term male newborn on day 3 who has not passed meconium since birth and now has bilious vomiting after each feed. He was born by uncomplicated vaginal birth at 39 weeks. His abdomen is progressively distended, and a digital rectal examination produces a forceful expulsion of meconium and gas. He has not had any prior abdominal surgery. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis, and the newborn screening blood spot is pending.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9, HR 158, RR 52, SpO2 98 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: Distended soft non-tender abdomen, visible bowel loops, no inguinal hernia, anus normally sited and patent
data_table.Hydration: Capillary refill 2 seconds, mucous membranes moist
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.36, lactate 2.1 mmol/L; supine abdominal radiograph shows dilated bowel loops with paucity of rectal gas

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

options.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy and intraoperative levelling biopsies under anaesthesia
options.rationale: Surgery is the definitive treatment after histological confirmation; proceeding without a confirmed diagnosis risks unnecessary laparotomy in alternative causes such as meconium plug or ileal atresia. (Source: RCH CPG: Hirschsprung disease)

options.text: Request a water-soluble contrast enema to characterise the transition zone in colonic anatomy
options.rationale: Contrast enema is supportive but neonates often lack a mature transition zone; its sensitivity in the first weeks is limited and it does not establish histological diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Hirschsprung disease)

options.text: Arrange a suction rectal biopsy for histological assessment of ganglion cells
options.rationale: Suction rectal biopsy showing absence of ganglion cells with hypertrophic nerve trunks is the definitive diagnostic test in neonates and can be done cot-side without anaesthesia. (Source: RCH CPG: Hirschsprung disease)

options.text: Trial high-dose oral lactulose and review the response over the next 48 hours
options.rationale: Stool softeners do not address aganglionic obstruction and risk worsening proximal distension with enterocolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Hirschsprung disease)

options.text: Send sweat chloride testing to exclude meconium ileus from cystic fibrosis
options.rationale: Sweat testing is unreliable before 2 weeks of age and the explosive release of stool on rectal examination is classic for Hirschsprung rather than meconium ileus. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic fibrosis)

explanation.summary: Hirschsprung disease presents in approximately 90 percent within the neonatal period with delayed meconium beyond 48 hours, abdominal distension and bilious vomiting. The classic squirt sign on rectal examination is highly suggestive. Suction rectal biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard. Contrast enema may identify a transition zone but is unreliable in neonates because the aganglionic segment has not yet hypertrophied proximally. Enterocolitis is the most dangerous early complication and warrants empiric antibiotics and rectal washouts.
key_points: Failure to pass meconium within 48 hours is a red flag
Suction rectal biopsy is the diagnostic test of choice
Histology shows absent ganglion cells with hypertrophic nerve trunks
Contrast enema sensitivity is limited in the neonatal period
Enterocolitis is the leading cause of mortality before surgery
explanation.pearls: Rectal biopsies must be taken at least 2 cm above the dentate line to avoid the physiologically hypoganglionic zone and a false-positive result.

subtopic_detail: Application of revised Jones criteria for acute rheumatic fever in high-risk populations
stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a regional Northern Territory hospital reviewing an 8-year-old Aboriginal girl with a 5-day history of migratory polyarthralgia and intermittent fevers. She had a sore throat 4 weeks ago that resolved without medical review. Her parents report she is no longer able to walk on the right knee. On examination she has a soft mid-systolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla and tender swelling of the right knee and left wrist.

data_table.Investigations: ESR 78 mm/hr, CRP 92 mg/L, ASOT 800 IU/mL, ECG PR interval 180 ms (age-corrected upper limit), throat swab pending
data_table.Echocardiography: Pending; transthoracic study available later today
data_table.Skin findings: No subcutaneous nodules or erythema marginatum identified

lead_in: Which diagnostic conclusion is best supported by the available findings under the 2015 revised Jones criteria?

options.text: Definite acute rheumatic fever can be diagnosed on the current clinical and laboratory findings
options.rationale: In high-risk populations the revised Jones criteria allow polyarthralgia plus carditis (auscultatory mitral regurgitation murmur) as two major manifestations, with elevated ASOT confirming preceding streptococcal infection. (Source: RHDAustralia 3rd edition guideline)

options.text: Probable acute rheumatic fever is the most that can be assigned without echocardiography confirmation
options.rationale: Clinical carditis on auscultation contributes a major criterion even before echocardiogram, although echocardiography should still be obtained urgently for severity assessment. (Source: RHDAustralia 3rd edition guideline)

options.text: Possible acute rheumatic fever is the appropriate label until subcutaneous nodules develop on review
options.rationale: Subcutaneous nodules are uncommon and not required; the case already meets the high-risk threshold for definite disease. (Source: RHDAustralia 3rd edition guideline)

options.text: Reactive arthritis is the more likely diagnosis given the migratory pattern of joint symptoms
options.rationale: Reactive arthritis would not produce a new murmur or ESR over 60, both of which fulfil rheumatic fever criteria. (Source: RHDAustralia 3rd edition guideline)

options.text: Septic arthritis must be excluded by joint aspiration before any rheumatic fever diagnosis
options.rationale: Septic arthritis is monoarticular and severely tender with a single hot joint; the migratory polyarthropathy and systemic features here do not fit. (Source: RHDAustralia 3rd edition guideline)

explanation.summary: The 2015 revised Jones criteria split populations into low-risk and high-risk. Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children from northern Australia are categorised as high-risk, in whom polyarthralgia counts as a major criterion and monoarthritis (with high-grade fever and elevated inflammatory markers) can also count. Two major, or one major plus two minor, manifestations plus evidence of streptococcal infection fulfils definite disease. Echocardiography is mandatory for severity grading and may upgrade clinical carditis or detect subclinical carditis. Lifelong benzathine penicillin G is the keystone of secondary prevention.
key_points: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children from northern Australia are high-risk for application of Jones criteria
High-risk thresholds count polyarthralgia and monoarthritis as major manifestations
Evidence of preceding streptococcal infection (ASOT, anti-DNase B, swab) is required
Echocardiography is mandatory for severity and may detect subclinical carditis
Benzathine penicillin G every 21-28 days is the standard secondary prophylaxis
explanation.pearls: ESR over 60 mm/hr or CRP over 30 mg/L counts as a minor criterion in the high-risk threshold; together with raised temperature over 38 degrees this scenario also satisfies two minor criteria.

subtopic_detail: Stepwise escalation of atopic eczema flare beyond moisturiser and mild topical steroid
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 3-year-old girl with a severe atopic eczema flare across the trunk and limbs. Her mother has applied liberal sorbolene and 1 percent hydrocortisone cream twice daily for 7 days with worsening lichenification and excoriation. There is no honey-coloured crusting, fever, or vesicular lesions. The family demonstrates good technique with moisturiser. Her sleep has been disturbed for 4 nights.

data_table.Skin examination: Diffuse erythema with lichenification at antecubital and popliteal fossae, excoriations, no pustules or oozing crust
data_table.Atopic background: Egg-allergy infancy, sibling with asthma, dust mite specific IgE strongly positive
data_table.Previous treatments: Sorbolene 3 times daily, 1 percent hydrocortisone twice daily for 7 days

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Continue 1 percent hydrocortisone twice daily and review the response again in 7 days
options.rationale: Mild steroid has failed after a full week; persisting risks ongoing skin damage and steroid phobia. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema)

options.text: Refer to a paediatric dermatologist for ciclosporin or methotrexate consideration
options.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for severe disease refractory to a moderate-potency topical steroid trial with wet wraps. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 5 days to settle the flare quickly
options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids cause rebound flare on cessation and are not recommended for routine atopic eczema flares. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema)

options.text: Step up to methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1 percent once daily with wet-wrap bandages overnight
options.rationale: Moderate-potency steroid plus wet wraps is the recommended escalation for moderate to severe flares not controlled on mild steroid, with rapid response and good safety in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema)

options.text: Switch to topical pimecrolimus twice daily as steroid-sparing maintenance therapy
options.rationale: Calcineurin inhibitors are indicated for sensitive areas or as steroid-sparing once a flare has settled; they are too slow as the primary flare treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema)

explanation.summary: Atopic eczema flares follow a stepwise approach: liberal emollients, low-potency topical corticosteroid, moderate-potency steroid with or without wet wraps, then specialist input for phototherapy or systemic therapy. Wet-wrap therapy uses a moist tubular layer over the steroid plus emollient to enhance penetration, cool the skin, and improve sleep. It is suitable for short courses of 3 to 5 nights. Steroid phobia is best addressed by demonstrating the fingertip unit and explaining that under-treatment causes more skin damage than appropriate steroid use.
key_points: Step up potency rather than persisting with an inadequate strength steroid
Methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1 percent is moderate-potency and once-daily
Wet wraps amplify steroid penetration and reduce excoriation
Oral corticosteroids cause rebound and are avoided in routine eczema
Calcineurin inhibitors are best for sensitive areas or maintenance
explanation.pearls: Methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1 percent applied once daily is approximately equivalent in potency to twice-daily betamethasone valerate 0.05 percent and is preferred in young children for the lower potency-to-effect ratio.

sources.label: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Atopic dermatitis

subtopic_detail: Renal monitoring and referral thresholds in Henoch-Schonlein purpura nephritis
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 6-year-old boy 4 weeks after a presentation with palpable purpura over the buttocks and lower limbs, abdominal pain, and bilateral ankle arthritis. He met clinical criteria for Henoch-Schonlein purpura and his skin and joint symptoms resolved within 10 days. His mother reports cola-coloured urine over the last 2 days. He is otherwise well, eating and drinking normally, with no oedema. The local emergency department sent a urine sample at the time of presentation that was negative for blood and protein.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 96, RR 20, BP 122/78 mmHg (>95th centile for height)
data_table.Blood results: Creatinine 78 micromol/L (baseline 35), urea 9.1 mmol/L, albumin 28 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the family and arrange a repeat urine dipstick and blood pressure check in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Nephrotic-range proteinuria with rising creatinine and hypertension is not benign and demands urgent specialist input rather than community follow-up. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for 2 weeks and review in clinic in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Steroids alone do not reliably prevent progression of established nephritis and starting without renal biopsy planning misses the diagnostic step that guides therapy. (Source: KHA-CARI: HSP nephritis)

options.text: Start oral aspirin and discharge with a 2-week clinic review for joint symptoms
options.rationale: Aspirin does not address established nephritis and the joint symptoms have already resolved. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Order renal ultrasound and discharge with general practitioner urinalysis monitoring weekly
options.rationale: Ultrasound rarely changes management in HSP nephritis and weekly GP monitoring fails to act on already established nephrotic-range disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Refer immediately to paediatric nephrology for inpatient assessment and renal biopsy planning
options.rationale: Nephrotic-range proteinuria with hypertension and an acute creatinine rise meets Australian criteria for urgent paediatric nephrology referral and biopsy to guide immunosuppression. (Source: KHA-CARI: HSP nephritis)

explanation.summary: Henoch-Schonlein purpura nephritis is the most important long-term complication of HSP and can develop weeks after the rash and joint symptoms have resolved. All children require monthly urinalysis and blood pressure monitoring for 6 months because nephritis can develop late. Mild urinary abnormalities are followed; nephrotic-range proteinuria, hypertension, acute kidney injury, or persistent moderate proteinuria warrant nephrology referral and biopsy. Treatment is guided by ISKDC histology grade and may include corticosteroids with mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide for crescentic disease.
key_points: Monthly urinalysis and BP for 6 months after HSP onset
Nephrotic-range proteinuria is urine protein creatinine ratio above 250 mg/mmol
Rising creatinine, nephrotic range proteinuria or hypertension trigger nephrology referral
Biopsy directs immunosuppressive intensity
Late onset of nephritis up to 6 months after rash is well-recognised
explanation.pearls: ISKDC grade 3 or higher crescentic HSP nephritis is the histological threshold above which Australian centres typically add mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide to prednisolone.

subtopic_detail: Non-IgE mediated cow milk protein allergy management ladder in infancy
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 5-month-old infant with a 6-week history of loose blood-streaked stools, eczema flare, and unsettled feeding. He is exclusively breastfed and his weight has fallen from the 50th to the 25th centile. There has been no acute reaction to any food, no hives, no swelling, and no vomiting. The mother has tried eliminating dairy from her own diet for 1 week with partial improvement. Skin prick testing to cow milk is negative.

data_table.Growth: Weight 6.5 kg (25th centile, dropping), length 65 cm (25th centile), head circ 42 cm (25th centile)
data_table.Stool examination: Mucus and visible blood streaks, no diarrhoea
data_table.Examination: Mild eczema in antecubital fossae, soft non-tender abdomen, no perianal fissure

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue maternal dairy exclusion strictly for at least 2 to 4 weeks with dietitian support
options.rationale: Non-IgE cow milk protein allergy in a breastfed infant is managed by complete maternal dairy elimination for a minimum 2 to 4 weeks before judging response, with calcium and vitamin D supplementation. (Source: ASCIA: Cow milk allergy)

options.text: Switch immediately to extensively hydrolysed formula and cease breastfeeding now
options.rationale: Cessation of breastfeeding is not required and is harmful when maternal elimination can be trialled successfully in non-IgE disease. (Source: ASCIA: Cow milk allergy)

options.text: Switch immediately to amino acid based formula given the bleeding per rectum
options.rationale: Amino acid formula is reserved for severe enteropathy, anaphylaxis history, or failure of extensively hydrolysed formula. (Source: ASCIA: Cow milk allergy)

options.text: Refer for paediatric gastroscopy and colonoscopy to exclude infantile inflammatory bowel disease
options.rationale: Inflammatory bowel disease is rare in this age group and the clinical picture fits non-IgE cow milk protein allergy; endoscopy is reserved for failure of elimination. (Source: ASCIA: Cow milk allergy)

options.text: Trial soy formula for 2 weeks and review symptoms in clinic at the end of the trial
options.rationale: Up to 30 percent of infants with cow milk protein allergy will also react to soy, so it is not a recommended first-line alternative. (Source: ASCIA: Cow milk allergy)

explanation.summary: Non-IgE mediated cow milk protein allergy commonly presents in the first 6 months with proctocolitis (blood-streaked mucousy stools in an otherwise well infant), enteropathy, or chronic GORD-like symptoms. Skin prick test is negative because it is non-IgE mediated. In breastfed infants, maternal cow milk exclusion is first line for at least 2 to 4 weeks with dietetic input and calcium plus vitamin D supplementation. If symptoms persist or the infant is formula-fed, extensively hydrolysed formula is next; amino acid formula is reserved for failure or severe disease. Cow milk should be reintroduced at 9 to 12 months to assess tolerance.
key_points: Non-IgE CMPA is diagnosed clinically; skin prick test is negative
Maternal dairy exclusion in breastfed infants is first line
Extensively hydrolysed formula is next when not breastfed
Amino acid formula is reserved for failure or severe disease
30 percent cross-react with soy; soy is not first line
explanation.pearls: Reintroduce cow milk at 9 to 12 months using the milk ladder to assess tolerance; up to 80 percent of children with non-IgE CMPA tolerate cow milk by age 3 years.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Cow milk protein allergy

subtopic_detail: Recognition and treatment of incomplete (atypical) Kawasaki disease in infants
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 9-month-old boy on day 6 of unexplained high fevers despite appropriate empiric ceftriaxone. He has a fine truncal rash, conjunctival injection without exudate, and red dry lips but no extremity changes, no cervical lymphadenopathy, and no oral cavity changes. Blood and urine cultures are sterile at 48 hours. Respiratory viral PCR is negative. Inflammatory markers are markedly elevated.

data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.4 x10^9/L, neutrophils 12.1 x10^9/L, platelets 540 x10^9/L, ALT 85 U/L, albumin 27 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue empiric ceftriaxone for a further 48 hours and review for clinical change
options.rationale: Persisting bacterial therapy alone delays Kawasaki recognition and increases the risk of coronary aneurysm beyond day 10. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Discharge with safety-net advice and arrange community paediatrician review in 1 week
options.rationale: Discharge before excluding Kawasaki disease in a febrile infant beyond day 5 is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Obtain echocardiography and treat with IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin given supplemental criteria are met
options.rationale: Infants under 12 months with prolonged unexplained fever and at least 3 supplemental laboratory criteria (CRP >=30, ESR >=40, anaemia, platelets >=450 after day 7, albumin <=30, sterile pyuria, raised ALT) meet incomplete Kawasaki criteria and receive IVIG plus aspirin; echo guides surveillance. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for 5 days as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroid monotherapy is not first line; adjunctive steroids are considered alongside IVIG in high-risk children, not as a stand-alone. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Order a chest computed tomography to investigate alternative infectious aetiologies
options.rationale: CT chest does not address the most likely diagnosis and exposes the infant to unnecessary radiation. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is most likely in infants under 12 months and carries the highest coronary aneurysm risk if missed. Diagnosis requires fever for at least 5 days plus 2 or 3 of the classical criteria, with supplemental laboratory criteria substituting for absent clinical features. IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion plus aspirin (high or moderate dose) should be given by day 10 to minimise coronary risk. Echocardiography is performed at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6 to 8 weeks. High-risk infants may benefit from concomitant corticosteroid or infliximab.
key_points: Infants under 12 months often present incompletely and have the highest coronary risk
Supplemental criteria: CRP, ESR, albumin, anaemia, late platelet rise, pyuria, ALT
IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin by day 10 is the standard treatment
Echocardiography at baseline, 2 weeks, and 6 to 8 weeks
Adjunct steroid or infliximab is considered in high-risk infants
explanation.pearls: Sterile pyuria in a febrile infant beyond day 5 is a classic Kawasaki clue and should not be misattributed to a partially-treated urinary tract infection.

sources.label: American Heart Association: Kawasaki disease scientific statement

subtopic_detail: Indications and timing of adenotonsillectomy in paediatric obstructive sleep apnoea
stem: You are the GP referred a 5-year-old girl by her preschool teacher for daytime inattention and morning headaches. Her parents describe loud nightly snoring with witnessed apnoeic pauses and restless sleep. She has dropped from the 50th to the 10th centile for weight. There is no rhinitis, no recurrent tonsillitis, and no atopic history. She has tonsils that touch the uvula and a Mallampati class III oropharynx.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 96, RR 22, BP 102/64 mmHg, SpO2 96 percent room air awake
data_table.Growth: Weight 16 kg (10th centile, falling), height 110 cm (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Mouth breathing, mild adenoidal facies, no inspiratory stridor, no rhinitis
data_table.Polysomnography (recent): Obstructive apnoea-hypopnoea index 8.4 per hour, nadir SpO2 87 percent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to ENT for adenotonsillectomy as the first-line treatment of moderate paediatric OSA
options.rationale: An obstructive apnoea-hypopnoea index above 5 with tonsillar hypertrophy is moderate paediatric OSA and Australian guidelines support adenotonsillectomy as first-line surgical management. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association: Paediatric OSA)

options.text: Trial intranasal mometasone for 6 weeks before any referral for surgical intervention
options.rationale: Intranasal corticosteroid is reserved for mild OSA (OAHI 1 to 5) or as adjunct in persistent symptoms after surgery; it is not appropriate for moderate OSA. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association: Paediatric OSA)

options.text: Commence trial of continuous positive airway pressure overnight while awaiting outpatient review
options.rationale: CPAP is the option when adenotonsillectomy is contraindicated, declined, or unsuccessful; it is not first-line in a typical child with tonsillar hypertrophy. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association: Paediatric OSA)

options.text: Order MRI airway to characterise the anatomy before any surgical referral is generated
options.rationale: Airway MRI is reserved for complex craniofacial syndromes or post-surgical persistent OSA, not initial assessment of typical adenotonsillar hypertrophy. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association: Paediatric OSA)

options.text: Reassure parents and arrange a sleep position trial with lateral positioning at night
options.rationale: Positional therapy is inadequate for moderate disease with daytime symptoms and growth faltering. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association: Paediatric OSA)

explanation.summary: Paediatric OSA is graded by the obstructive apnoea-hypopnoea index: 1 to 5 mild, 5 to 10 moderate, above 10 severe. Adenotonsillar hypertrophy is the leading cause. Adenotonsillectomy is first-line for moderate and severe disease in otherwise well children, with cure rates of around 70 to 80 percent. Children with persistent symptoms after surgery, contraindications, or syndromic airways need CPAP. Untreated OSA causes daytime inattention, behavioural disturbance, growth faltering (from disrupted growth hormone secretion), enuresis, and in severe cases pulmonary hypertension.
key_points: OAHI 5 to 10 is moderate paediatric OSA
Adenotonsillectomy is first line in tonsillar hypertrophy
Intranasal steroid is for mild OSA, not moderate or severe
CPAP is second line after surgery failure or contraindication
Growth faltering can reverse after successful surgery
explanation.pearls: Children under 3 years, severe OSA, or significant comorbidity warrant overnight inpatient monitoring after adenotonsillectomy because of higher rates of respiratory complications.

subtopic_detail: Distinguishing infective mastitis from breast abscess in lactating women
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 30-year-old woman who is 5 weeks postpartum, exclusively breastfeeding her first baby. She presents with 36 hours of right breast pain, redness, fevers and rigors. She started oral flucloxacillin 24 hours ago after a phone triage with the practice nurse. She remains febrile and now describes a tender lump in the upper outer quadrant that did not improve after a full breastfeed or hand expression.

data_table.Examination: Right upper outer quadrant 4 cm fluctuant tender mass with overlying erythema, no nipple discharge, lactating left breast unremarkable
data_table.Lactation: Baby feeding well from left breast, refused right breast in last 12 hours

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Switch oral antibiotic to oral cephalexin and review the response in 48 hours
options.rationale: Antibiotic switching alone does not drain an abscess and delays definitive management. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Add oral metronidazole to the current flucloxacillin and continue feeding from both breasts
options.rationale: Anaerobic cover is not indicated in standard puerperal mastitis or abscess and adds no benefit. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Cease breastfeeding from the affected breast and commence cabergoline to suppress lactation
options.rationale: Continued emptying of the breast is essential to recovery; suppression of lactation worsens the situation and is not indicated. (Source: ABM Clinical Protocol 36: Mastitis)

options.text: Refer to emergency department for breast ultrasound and needle aspiration if collection confirmed
options.rationale: A fluctuant mass that has not improved on 24 hours of appropriate antibiotic requires ultrasound-guided needle aspiration of any confirmed collection, with breastfeeding continued. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Breastfeeding)

options.text: Arrange urgent surgical incision and drainage with general anaesthesia for the suspected collection
options.rationale: Incision and drainage is reserved for collections that fail repeated needle aspiration or large skin necrosis; needle aspiration is the contemporary first step. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Breastfeeding)

explanation.summary: Puerperal mastitis is initially managed with effective breast emptying, analgesia, and oral flucloxacillin or cephalexin (or clindamycin if penicillin allergic). Failure to improve within 24 to 48 hours, a persistent tender lump, or fluctuance suggests abscess and warrants ultrasound. Modern management uses ultrasound-guided needle aspiration and may need repeat aspiration over several days, avoiding surgical incision in most cases. Breastfeeding from the affected breast continues whenever the baby will accept it; if not, hand expression or pumping prevents milk stasis.
key_points: First-line antibiotic is flucloxacillin or cephalexin
Failure to improve at 24 to 48 hours raises suspicion of abscess
Ultrasound confirms collection; needle aspiration is first line
Breastfeeding should continue from the affected breast
Incision and drainage is for failed aspiration or necrosis
explanation.pearls: MRSA cover (clindamycin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) is added when there is no improvement on first-line therapy or when local MRSA prevalence is high, especially in the Northern Territory.

subtopic_detail: Risk-based versus universal screening approach to intrapartum GBS prophylaxis
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 32-year-old G2P1 woman at 39 weeks of gestation in early spontaneous labour. She had a vaginal swab at 36 weeks which grew group B streptococcus. Her first baby was delivered uncomplicated 3 years ago. There is no history of penicillin allergy or anaphylaxis. Her membranes ruptured 2 hours ago with clear liquor and she is 4 cm dilated with regular contractions.

data_table.CTG: Baseline 138 bpm, normal variability, accelerations present, no decelerations
data_table.Examination: Cephalic, station -1, cervix 4 cm dilated, 70 percent effaced, clear liquor
data_table.Allergy status: No known drug allergies; previous penicillin courses tolerated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery
options.rationale: Australian guidelines recommend benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth for GBS-positive women in labour without penicillin allergy. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin 2 g 8-hourly until delivery is the recommended first-line regimen
options.rationale: Cefazolin is reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy; benzylpenicillin remains first line when no allergy exists. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 900 mg 8-hourly until delivery is the appropriate alternative regimen
options.rationale: Clindamycin is for severe penicillin allergy with confirmed clindamycin susceptibility of the isolate. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g every 6 hours until delivery is the most practical regimen now
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not reach adequate intrapartum concentrations and are not used for GBS intrapartum prophylaxis. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

options.text: Withhold antibiotics and re-swab now for repeat GBS PCR before any treatment decision
options.rationale: Antenatal screening at 35 to 37 weeks is sufficient; repeat swabbing in labour delays effective prophylaxis. (Source: RANZCOG GBS guideline)

explanation.summary: Australia uses a hybrid screening approach for early-onset group B streptococcal disease prevention. Universal antenatal swabbing at 35 to 37 weeks is widely adopted; risk-based prophylaxis is the alternative pathway. Indications for intrapartum prophylaxis include positive antenatal swab, GBS bacteriuria in this pregnancy, previous infant with invasive GBS disease, or any of (preterm labour, prolonged rupture of membranes over 18 hours, intrapartum fever 38 degrees or higher) when GBS status is unknown. Benzylpenicillin is first line, cefazolin if non-severe penicillin allergy, clindamycin (after susceptibility) for severe allergy, and vancomycin for resistant or severely allergic patients.
key_points: Australian screening occurs at 35 to 37 weeks gestation
Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV load then 1.8 g 4-hourly is first line
Cefazolin replaces it for non-severe penicillin allergy
Clindamycin (with susceptibility) replaces it for severe allergy
Adequate prophylaxis requires at least 4 hours before delivery
explanation.pearls: Vancomycin is the back-up for severe penicillin allergy when clindamycin susceptibility is not confirmed or the isolate is resistant.
